questions.q: When Davisson and Germer fired electrons at their nickel target, what observation revealed wave behaviour?
options: The electrons came off in a smooth, uniform spray in all directions
The electrons emerged in sharp peaks at specific angles with dead zones between them, the signature of interference
The electrons were absorbed entirely by the nickel
The electrons gained energy on reflection
questions.explanation: Particles would scatter in a smooth spray, but the electrons piled up in sharp peaks at particular angles with gaps between — constructive and destructive interference, the unmistakable signature of waves.

questions.q: What was de Broglie's central idea?
options: Light is only ever a wave, never a particle
Electrons have no momentum
If a wave (light) can behave as a particle, then a particle (matter) can behave as a wave — duality is universal
Only photons can have a wavelength
questions.explanation: De Broglie turned wave-particle duality around: since light, thought to be a wave, also acts as a particle, matter thought to be particles should also have a wave nature. The duality runs both ways and is universal.

questions.q: The de Broglie wavelength of a particle is given by:
options: \(\lambda = h m v\)
\(\lambda = \dfrac{h}{m v}\)
\(\lambda = \dfrac{m v}{h}\)
\(\lambda = \dfrac{h v}{m}\)
questions.explanation: \(\lambda = h/p = h/(mv)\): wavelength equals Planck's constant divided by momentum. Wavelength is inversely proportional to momentum, so heavy, fast objects have undetectably tiny wavelengths.

questions.q: Why can a nickel crystal diffract a 54 V electron but a doorway cannot diffract a walking person?
options: The crystal is made of metal and the doorway is not
A 54 V electron's de Broglie wavelength (~0.17 nm) is comparable to the atomic spacing, whereas a person's wavelength is ~\(10^{-35}\) m — vastly smaller than any opening
Electrons are charged and people are not
People move too slowly to have any wavelength
questions.explanation: Diffraction is only observable when the wavelength is comparable to the slit spacing. A 54 V electron has a wavelength near the atomic spacing (~0.1 nm), so a crystal diffracts it; a person's de Broglie wavelength is around \(10^{-35}\) m, far too small for any opening to reveal.

questions.q: Why is the agreement between the predicted and measured wavelengths in the Davisson-Germer experiment considered strong proof?
options: Both numbers came from de Broglie's equation
De Broglie's theory (knowing nothing of the experiment) predicted ~0.167 nm, while the diffraction geometry (knowing nothing of de Broglie) gave ~0.165 nm — two independent roads to the same value
The numbers were rounded to match
The experiment was repeated thousands of times by the same method
questions.explanation: One road started from the accelerating voltage and de Broglie's formula; the other used only the measured angle and the known nickel atomic spacing. They agreed to better than 2%, two completely independent derivations converging — that is what proof looks like.

questions.q: How did G.P. Thomson's experiment differ from Davisson and Germer's, and why was this important?
options: He used the same method, confirming nothing new
He fired fast electrons through thin metal films (transmission), getting concentric rings rather than reflected peaks — a different geometry reaching the same de Broglie wavelength
He used light instead of electrons
He fired protons instead of electrons
questions.explanation: Thomson transmitted fast electrons through thin polycrystalline films, producing concentric rings, while Davisson-Germer reflected slow electrons off a single crystal, producing peaks. Two utterly different geometries reaching the same de Broglie wavelength removed any doubt.

questions.q: The poetic twist in this story is that:
options: Davisson and Thomson were brothers
J.J. Thomson proved the electron was a particle, and his son George Paget Thomson proved the same electron was a wave
De Broglie later disproved his own idea
Einstein performed the electron-diffraction experiment himself
questions.explanation: J.J. Thomson discovered the electron and showed it was a particle (Nobel 1906); his son G.P. Thomson showed the electron was a wave (Nobel 1937). One family, two generations, two prizes — one for the particle, one for the wave.

questions.q: How do de Broglie matter waves explain why only certain electron orbits are allowed in an atom?
options: The electron randomly chooses an orbit each time
A stable orbit must contain a whole number of de Broglie wavelengths around its circumference, forming a standing wave; other orbits self-destruct by destructive interference
The nucleus pushes the electron into fixed orbits
Electrons can occupy any orbit at all
questions.explanation: Only orbits whose circumference fits a whole number of wavelengths (\(2\pi r = n\lambda\)) form a stable standing wave. Substituting de Broglie's wavelength yields Bohr's angular-momentum rule — derived rather than merely assumed.

questions.q: Substituting \(\lambda = h/(mv)\) into the standing-wave condition \(2\pi r = n\lambda\) gives what well-known result?
options: Bohr's quantisation of angular momentum, \(mvr = nh/2\pi\)
Newton's law of gravitation
The photoelectric equation
questions.explanation: Substituting \(\lambda = h/(mv)\) into \(2\pi r = n\lambda\) rearranges to \(mvr = nh/2\pi\) — exactly Bohr's quantisation postulate, now derived from the standing-wave picture instead of assumed.

questions.q: When electrons are fired one at a time through a crystal and still build up an interference pattern, what does each electron interfere with?
options: The other electrons in the beam
Itself — each single electron's wave explores all paths and interferes with itself
The atoms of the detector
Nothing; the pattern is an illusion
questions.explanation: Even fired one at a time with only ever one electron in the apparatus, an interference pattern still builds up dot by dot. Each electron interferes with itself: its wave (a wave of probability, per Born) explores all paths, and the electron lands as a particle at one point.
